coredata.dc:title: Direct determination of graphene quantum dots based on terbium-sensitized luminescence
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Llorent-Martínez, Eulogio J.
Molina-García, Lucía
Durán, Gema M.
Ruiz-Medina, Antonio
Ríos, Ángel
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) were determined in water samples using terbium-sensitized luminescence (TSL). Terbium ions complex with GQD due to the carboxylic groups that are usually present in these nanomaterials, increasing the luminescence signal of terbium. In Tb(III)-GQD complexes, GQD absorb energy at their characteristic excitation wavelength and transfer it to terbium ion, which emits at its particular emission wavelength. The analytical signal, measured at λexc =257nm and λem =545nm, increases proportionally to GQD concentration between 50 and 500μgL−1. Under optimum conditions, the proposed method presents a detection limit of 15μgL−1 and is selective to GQD in the presence of other nanomaterials of similar size. As GQD are highly water-soluble, they are potential contaminants in environmental or drinking waters water samples, and hence the method was applied to the analysis of different drinking waters which were the target samples for the application of the developed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Terbium
Time-resolved luminescence
Nanomaterials
Graphene quantum dots
Water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the TSL mechanism for the determination of GQD.
Excitation and emission luminescence spectra of Tb(III)-GQD. Excitation (A) and emission (B) fluorescence spectra of Tb(III)-GQD.
Influence of the Tb(III) concentration in the intensity of emission luminiscence fluorescence (500μgL−1 GQD).
Influence of the pH in the intensity of emission luminescence (300μgL−1 GQD).
Interference study for the TSL determination of GQD.

head.ce:title: Direct determination of graphene quantum dots based on terbium-sensitized luminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry and Food Technology, University of Castilla-La Mancha, Av. Camilo José Cela, Ciudad Real E-13071, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical and Analytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) were determined in water samples using terbium-sensitized luminescence (TSL). Terbium ions complex with GQD due to the carboxylic groups that are usually present in these nanomaterials, increasing the luminescence signal of terbium. In Tb(III)-GQD complexes, GQD absorb energy at their characteristic excitation wavelength and transfer it to terbium ion, which emits at its particular emission wavelength. The analytical signal, measured at λexc =257nm and λem =545nm, increases proportionally to GQD concentration between 50 and 500μgL−1. Under optimum conditions, the proposed method presents a detection limit of 15μgL−1 and is selective to GQD in the presence of other nanomaterials of similar size. As GQD are highly water-soluble, they are potential contaminants in environmental or drinking waters water samples, and hence the method was applied to the analysis of different drinking waters which were the target samples for the application of the developed method.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has experienced exponential growth in the last few years due to the advantages of nanomaterials, which present specific characteristics derived from their size. Engineered nanoparticles show significant applications in a wide range of fields. For instance, recent advances have been reported in medicine [1,2], industrial applications [1,3], or catalysis [4]. It is thus inevitable that during their production and use they may be released into the environment, either intentionally or by accident [5]. In 2006, concerns about the potential toxicity of nanomaterials started to arise [6,7]. About a decade ago, most of the works were oriented to the synthesis and characterization of new nanomaterials, but rarely to their quantitative analysis in environmental systems [8]. Although more analytical methodologies have been recently developed, this research field is still relatively unexploited.

GQD are a type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials sharing characteristics from both graphene and carbon dots. Their diameters are usually between 3 and 20nm, and they present unique characteristics because of the quantum confinement and edge effects, which are similar to carbon dots [9,10]. GQD have excellent mechanical, thermal, optical, and electrical properties. They also show low cytotoxicity, water-solubility, chemical inertia, and stable photoluminescence [10]. Due to these favorable characteristics, there has been a constant increase in GQD applications. In particular, research on GQD has focused mainly on two aspects: development of different syntheses and evaluation of new applications [11]. However, although new uses and future trends for the applications of GQD are regularly reported, the development of analytical methods for their determination is not evolving at the same pace – a similar pattern is observed for other nanomaterials. To our best knowledge, only a fluorescence method has been reported for the determination of GQD [12]. Thus, we propose here an alternative sensitive and selective analytical method for the determination of these nanomaterials.

Typically, GQD contain carboxylic groups, which makes them good candidates to form complexes with terbium ions. This characteristic can thus be exploited to use terbium-sensitized luminescence (TSL) detection. In TSL, the analyte absorbs the energy and transfers it to terbium ion. Then, Tb (III) produces the emission, which is the analytical signal recorded. The excitation and emission wavelengths are characteristic of the analyte and Tb(III), respectively [13]. The main features of TSL are a large Stokes shift, a narrow-band emission, and a long luminescence lifetime. The latter makes it possible to use the phosphorescence mode to record the analytical signals, achieving high sensitivity and selectivity.

The World Health Organization is concerned about the increasing use of nanoparticles, which are listed among the key environment and health challenges due to their potential risks to health. Although GQD are considered of low toxicity, there are not enough research studies to confirm this assumption. For instance, researchers reported that GQD accumulate in the liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor sites of mice after intravenous injections of GQD [14]. Although no appreciable toxicity occurred, moderate pathological changes were observed in the liver and lung. It is thus important to develop reliable analytical methodologies for the analysis of these nanomaterials to be able to control their levels. Taking into account the high solubility of GQD in water, we propose a TSL analytical method for the determination of GQD in drinking waters. This method presents high selectivity to GQD in the presence of other nanomaterials, including fluorescent carbon dots. The analytical features and recovery yields of the method show its suitability as an alternative for the determination of nanoparticles with a straightforward and low-cost procedure.

Luminescence measurements were recorded in a Cary-Eclipse Luminescence Spectrometer (Varian Inc., Mulgrave, Australia) with Cary-Eclipse (Varian) software and a Hellma 1010-QS quartz cuvette (3500μL volume, 10×10mm light path). A Sonorex Digital 10P (Bandelin Electronic, Berlin, Germany) ultrasonic bath and a Crison GLP 21 pH-meter (Crison Instruments, Barcelona, Spain) were also used.

A magnetic stirrer hot plate (VWR, Pittburgh, PA, USA) and a microcentrifuge Biosan Microspin 12 (LabNet Biotecnica S.L., Spain) were used for GQD synthesis. UV–Vis absorbance spectra were acquired with a SECOMAM UVI Light XS2 spectrophotometer (SECOMAM, Champigny/Marne, France) equipped with a LabPower V3 50 for absorbance data acquisition. Details of the complete instrumentation used for the synthesis and characterization of GQD are detailed in a previous paper [15].
All reagents and standards were of analytical reagent grade. We used ultrapure water (Milli-Q Waters purification system; Millipore; Milford, MA) in all experiments.

Carbon dots (quantum efficiency >5%, ≥0.2% in H2O, λem 450–550nm), silica nanopowder (SiO2, 12nm; 99.8%), and titanium oxide (TiO2, anatase, 25nm; 99.7%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Madrid, Spain). TiO2 (5nm, anatase) and TiO2 (rutile, 60nm; 99.8%) were bought from Nanostructured & Amorphous Materials, Inc. (Houston, Texas, USA). 5mgmL−1 SiO2 solution (20nm) was purchased from nanoComposix (Prague, Czech Republic).

Uric acid (≥ 99.0%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Sulfuric acid (ACS/FCC, BDH ARISTAR, 95.0–98%) and acetone (Hipersolv Chromanorm, 100%) were acquired from VWR (Pittsburgh, PA, USA); nitric acid (HNO3; ≥69%) was supplied by Labkem (Mataró, Barcelona, Spain). Acetic acid (99.7%), sodium hydroxide (≥99%), hydrochloric acid (37%), and methanol (99.9%) were purchased from Panreac (Barcelona, Spain). Terbium (III) nitrate pentahydrate (99.9%), sodium acetate (≥99%), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (98%), and interferent compounds were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.
Stock solutions of Tb3+ (0.1molL−1) and GQD (1000mgL−1) were prepared in ultrapure water. They were kept at room temperature in the dark and remained stable for at least one month.

All solutions were prepared in 10mL volumetric flasks, completing to volume with ultrapure water. For standard solutions, the required volumes of Tb3+ and GQD solutions were added to obtain 10−4 M Tb3+ and 50–500μgL−1 GQD concentrations. The same procedure was used for samples by adding appropriate volumes of water samples instead of GQD solution. Sample preparation only required filtration and dilution steps. In all cases, we recorded a blank solution of 10−4 M Tb3+.

Emission luminescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 257nm, using peak height at 545nm as the analytical signal. The net analytical signal (analytical signal from now on) was the signal from the standard/sample solution minus the signal from the Tb(III) blank. The following instrumental conditions were used: excitation/emission slit widths, 10/20nm/nm; photomultiplier voltage, 850V; delay time, 0.1ms; gate time, 3ms.

GQD were obtained following a methodology previously reported [15]. The synthesis procedure was based on the acidic-based chemical treatment of 0.5g of uric acid with 1mL of concentrated sulfuric acid at 200°C for 1h, their subsequent neutralization with NaOH and their purification with a mixture of methanol and acetone (1:4, v:v). Then, the obtained GQD were filtered through a 0.45μm microporous membrane to separate large graphene quantum dots nanoparticles and the yellow product was dried under N2 flow and stored protected from light at room temperature. The resulting GQD presented a narrow size distribution of 2.5–4.8nm, with an average size of 3.7nm. Maximum absorbance was observed at 255nm, with fluorescence excitation wavelengths of 257 and 360nm, and an emission wavelength of 455nm. The complete characterization of GQD – Raman, FTIR, X-ray diffraction, etc. has been previously detailed [15].

GQD were obtained using uric acid powder as the single precursor by an acidic-based procedure previously reported in our laboratory [15]. The complete characterization of GQD is detailed in the mentioned article.

The proposed TSL method is based on the formation of the complex Tb(III)-GQD. In the absence of GQD, terbium ions produce a weak emission signal. However, when Tb(III)-GQD complexes are formed – by the reaction between Tb(III) and the carboxylic groups present in GQD – the analytical signal increases (Fig. 1 ), proportionally to GQD concentration.

Firstly, the excitation and emission luminescence spectra of Tb(III)-GQD were recorded (Fig. 2 ) in order to select the optimum wavelengths. GQD presents excitation wavelengths of 257 and 360nm (data not shown), with the most intense band at 360nm. However, in the presence of Tb3+, the intensity of the bands is reversed. In this case, the most intense excitation band in Tb(III)-GQD was selected (257nm, as Fig. 2 shows). The emission wavelength of 545nm corresponds to terbium 5D4 → 7F5 transition, which is the most sensitive emission band in terbium [13].

Then, the delay (0.1–0.3ms) and gate (1–5ms) times were optimized. The analytical signal diminished as the delay time increased. On the contrary, the signal increased up to 3ms gate time, slightly decreasing for higher values. Therefore, delay and gate times of 0.1 and 3ms, respectively, were selected. Finally, the excitation/emission slit widths and the PMT voltage were optimized. Wide slit widths and high PMT voltages increased the analytical signal. However, the signal from the Tb(III) blank has to be kept as low as possible, to obtain a proper linear dynamic range. In this case, a compromise between sensitivity and linear range was adopted. The final conditions were excitation/emission slit widths of 10/20nm/nm and PMT of 850V. Although these wide slit widths can be a source of potential interferences if fluorescence is used, the use of delayed luminescence avoids the interference from co-existing fluorescence compounds. In addition, slit widths are usually wider in delayed luminescence than fluorescence.

The most critical parameter was terbium concentration, which was optimized using aqueous solutions simultaneously with slit widths and PMT voltage, as the blank signal had to be considered for all the studied parameters. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . It can be observed that the optimum Tb3+ concentration was 1×10−4 molL−1, obtaining lower analytical signals at higher and lower terbium concentrations.

In TSL, the analytical signal highly depends on the pH of the solutions. The influence of the pH in the luminescence signal is shown in Fig. 4 . For Low pH values lower than 5, the analytical signal drastically decreased due to the protonation of the carboxylic groups, hence the absence of GQD-Tb(III) complexes. (lower than 5) decreased the analytical signal, probably due to the protonation of the carboxylic groups, hence the absence of GQD-Tb(III) complexes. Moreover, the hydrolysis of terbium takes place for pH values higher than 8–9. As it is observed in Fig. 4, Therefore, pH values between 5.5 and 8 produced the best analytical signal. Finally, acetate and phosphate buffers were tested at different concentrations (0.02–0.2molL−1) within this pH range. As there was no improvement in the analytical signal, a simple non-buffered aqueous solution of 1×10−4 molL−1 Tb3+ was used. All analyzed samples were within the optimum pH range.

This work aimed to propose a novel analytical method for the determination of GQD in waters, paying particular attention to the potential interference of other nanomaterials. Hence, we calculated the tolerated interferent/analyte ratio for several nanoparticles. Among carbon nanoparticles, carbon dots were selected because they are also water-soluble fluorescent nanoparticles. However, other common carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, are not soluble in water and should not be present at measurable levels. Silica and titanium dioxide nanoparticles are used as food additives. Titanium dioxide is also used in sun creams, whereas gold nanoparticles are found in different cosmetics. The common use of these nanoparticles may lead to their presence in environmental or drinking waters. The possible interference of different inorganic ions that are likely to be present in water samples was also studied. It was assumed that no interference took place if the variation of the signal was lower than 4% - the RSD in the absence of any interfering compound – after the addition of a potential interferent. Results are summarized in Table 1 . It can be observed that the studied nanoparticles did not produce interference in the analytical signal produced by GQD. These nanoparticles were selected not only due to their common use but also to their similar (5–20nm) or slightly higher (20–60nm) size. Also, high levels of common ions in water samples produced no interference as well. As a result, the selective interaction between Tb3+ and GQD allows the analysis of water samples.

The selectivity of the method was assessed in the previous section. In addition, we calculated the detection and quantitation limits, the calibration graph, and the precision of the analytical method under the optimum conditions. In all cases, peak height was used as the analytical signal. The analytical signal was dependent on GQD concentration, increasing linearly. To define the calibration curve, a blank solution (1×10−4 molL−1 Tb3+) and eight standard solutions at different GQD concentrations (50–500μgL−1) were used. Time-resolved luminescence spectra were recorded. The analytical signal was net luminescence signal (standard solution signal minus Tb3+ signal), and we fitted data by standard least-squares treatment. The proposed analytical methodology presents detection and quantitation limits (3σ and 10 σ criterion) of 15 and 50μgL−1 GQD, respectively, and a linear range between 50 and 500μgL−1 GQD. This detection limit is close to that previously reported in a fluorescent method [12]. However, no sample pre-treatment is required in the present work, hence simplifying the methodology. The repeatability and intermediate precision (3 non-consecutive days; calculated using spiked spring water) were lower than 4 and 7%, respectively. The accuracy is assessed in Section 3.5. A summary of the analytical parameters is shown in Table 2 .

Finally, the robustness of the method was evaluated making small changes to some of the chemical and instrumental parameters: excitation wavelength (±2nm), PMT voltage (±10V), and Tb (III) concentration (±5%). The changes in the analytical signal were lower than 4% compared to the optimum conditions, hence demonstrating the robustness of the method.

We aimed to propose an analytical method for the determination of GQD in water samples, paying particular attention to drinking waters. Consequently, three different types of water samples were selected: tap, mineral, and spring waters. Taking into account that GQD were not present in the samples, we carried out recovery studies at several levels (Table 3 ). Only filtration and dilution steps were necessary before analysis.

Chong et al. reported very low in vivo and in vitro toxicity of GQD [16]. Similarly, low toxicity was also reported for other carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots. In addition, Jiang et al. observed no toxicity for concentrations as high as 20mgmL−1 [17]. Taking into account the low toxicity of GQD, the presence of traces of these nanomaterials in drinking water would not represent a threat to human health. Hence, the samples were spiked at QDs concentrations between 1 and 20mgL−1, and they were analyzed in triplicate. Recoveries between 90 and 100% were obtained, with RSDs values of 2–4% in all cases.

To evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method, we used the method of the averaged recovery [18]. The mean recovery was calculated from the individual recoveries (Table 3). This value was tested for significance using the Student t-test. An experimental t value of 0.842 (n =9) was obtained, which was lower than the tabulated t value, 1.860 (n-1 degrees of freedom; 95% confidence level). Hence, it can be assumed that the proposed method is accurate.

In this work we have proposed, to our best knowledge, the first analytical method based on TSL for the determination of nanoparticles. Also, this is the second analytical method reported for the quantification of GQD. The use of TSL has permitted the selective determination of GQD - some of the most widely used nanomaterials at the moment – in the presence of other nanomaterials. Considering the low toxicity of GQD, the sensitivity of the developed method represents an attractive approach for the determination of these nanoparticles in drinking waters. The method is selective, as the study of the potential interference of other common nanomaterials that may be present in water samples demonstrated. In addition, one important advantage of the method is the absence of any sample pre-treatment for the analysis of water samples. Hence, it can be used as a direct-screening method for GQD in waters, and it can be modified for the analysis of more complex samples by using appropriate sample treatments.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The following funding is acknowledged: CTQ2016-78793-P and CTQ2016-7511-R (Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitiveness, MINECO), PEIC-2014-001-P (Junta de Comunidades de Castilla-La Mancha, JCCM) and DIPUCR-16-LLORENTMARTINEZ (Diputación de Ciudad Real).
